text:
```go saga-os-go/internal/handler/routes/routes.go
package routes

import (
	"time"

	"github.com/go-chi/chi/v5"
	"github.com/go-chi/chi/v5/middleware"
	"github.com/go-chi/httprate"
	"github.com/go-chi/jwtauth/v5"

	"github.com/brandsoy/sagaware-server/config/env"
	"github.com/brandsoy/sagaware-server/internal/handler"
	custommiddleware "github.com/brandsoy/sagaware-server/internal/handler/middleware"
)

func InitAuthRoutes(router chi.Router, h handler.Handler) {
	router.Route("/auth", func(r chi.Router) {
		r.Post("/login", h.Login)
	})
}

func InitUserRoutes(router chi.Router, h handler.Handler) {
	router.Route("/users", func(r chi.Router) {
		r.Post("/", h.CreateUser)
		r.Patch("/{userId}", h.UpdateUser)
		r.Get("/{userId}", h.GetUserByID)
		r.Delete("/{userId}", h.DeleteUser)
		r.Patch("/{userId}/password", h.UpdateUserPassword)
		r.Get("/", h.FindManyUsers)
	})
}

func InitRoutes(router chi.Router, h handler.Handler) {

	router.Use(middleware.RequestID)
	router.Use(middleware.RealIP)
	router.Use(middleware.Logger)
	router.Use(middleware.Recoverer)
	router.Use(httprate.LimitByIP(100, 1*time.Minute))

	router.Use(custommiddleware.LoggerData)

	InitAuthRoutes(router, h)

	router.Route("/api/v1", func(r chi.Router) {
		r.Use(jwtauth.Verifier(env.Env.TokenAuth))
		r.Use(jwtauth.Authenticator(env.Env.TokenAuth))

		InitUserRoutes(r, h)

	})
}
```

look at the code here. Does the routing setup look good to you?
The routing setup looks generally well-structured and follows good practices. Here are the positive points and some potential suggestions:

Positive Points:
1. Uses Chi router which is a lightweight and efficient router
2. Implements essential middleware:
   - Request ID tracking
   - Real IP detection
   - Logging
   - Panic recovery
   - Rate limiting
3. Properly separates routes into logical groups (auth and users)
4. Implements versioning (/api/v1)
5. JWT authentication is implemented for protected routes
6. RESTful naming conventions are followed

Suggestions for improvement:
1. You might want to consider adding CORS middleware if this API will be accessed from different origins
2. Could add request timeout middleware to prevent long-running requests
3. Consider grouping related user endpoints more specifically (e.g., separate password operations)
4. Might want to add health check endpoint for monitoring
5. Could consider adding compression middleware for larger responses

Here's how you might enhance it:

```go
func InitRoutes(router chi.Router, h handler.Handler) {
    // Basic middleware
    router.Use(middleware.RequestID)
    router.Use(middleware.RealIP)
    router.Use(middleware.Logger)
    router.Use(middleware.Recoverer)
    router.Use(middleware.Timeout(30 * time.Second))
    router.Use(middleware.Compress(5))
    router.Use(cors.Handler(cors.Options{
        // Configure CORS options
    }))
    router.Use(httprate.LimitByIP(100, 1*time.Minute))
    router.Use(custommiddleware.LoggerData)

    // Health check
    router.Get("/health", h.HealthCheck)

    // Auth routes (public)
    InitAuthRoutes(router, h)

    // Protected routes
    router.Route("/api/v1", func(r chi.Router) {
        r.Use(jwtauth.Verifier(env.Env.TokenAuth))
        r.Use(jwtauth.Authenticator(env.Env.TokenAuth))

        InitUserRoutes(r, h)
        // Other protected routes...
    })
}
```

Overall, the current implementation is solid, and these suggestions are just potential enhancements depending on your specific needs.
Help with this error : > make swagger
swag init -o ./docs
make[1]: swag: No such file or directory
make[1]: *** [swagger] Error 1
failed to execute pre_cmd: exit status 2

This error occurs because the `swag` CLI tool is not installed on your system. To fix this, you need to install the Swag tool first. Here's how you can do it:

1. Install Swag using Go:
```bash
go install github.com/swaggo/swag/cmd/swag@latest
```

2. Make sure your Go bin directory is in your PATH. Add this to your ~/.bashrc or ~/.zshrc:
```bash
export PATH=$(go env GOPATH)/bin:$PATH
```

3. Source your shell configuration file or restart your terminal:
```bash
source ~/.bashrc
# or
source ~/.zshrc
```

4. Verify the installation:
```bash
swag -v
```

After installing Swag, you should be able to run `make swagger` successfully.

If you're using Go modules (go.mod), you might also want to add these dependencies to your project:
```bash
go get -u github.com/swaggo/swag/cmd/swag
go get -u github.com/swaggo/gin-swagger
go get -u github.com/swaggo/files
```

Now the `make swagger` command should work properly.
